Tutorial: Week 5 — Geometry of Rank and Rank Inequalities ​

For: Teaching Assistants conducting tutorial sessions (Mar 26, 2026) Related Lectures: Lecture 5: Solving Equations and Null Space, Lecture 6: Four Fundamental SubspacesDuration: 50 minutes Prerequisites: Cross-filling, rank, left/right cancellation, Col(AB)⊆Col(A), Null(B)⊆Null(AB), four fundamental subspaces, Col(AT)⊥Null(A)

Problem 1: The Rank Formula for VU ​

Let U be m×r with rank(U)=r and V be r×m with rank(V)=r.

(a) Show that rank(UV)=r.

Idea: U is left cancelable, so Null(UV)=Null(V). Then use rank-nullity.

Proof

Since rank(U)=r equals the number of columns of U, we have Null(U)={0} (left cancelable). By Lecture 6 Theorem 3, Null(U)∩Col(V)={0} implies Null(UV)=Null(V).

By rank-nullity on V (r×m): dim⁡(Null(V))=m−r.

By rank-nullity on UV (m×m): rank(UV)=m−dim⁡(Null(UV))=m−(m−r)=r. ∎

Now flip the order: VU is r×r (square). What is its rank?

(b) Prove the following rank formula:

rank(VU)=r−dim⁡(Null(V)∩Col(U))

Idea: Show that x↦Ux gives a one-to-one correspondence between Null(VU) and Null(V)∩Col(U), then use rank-nullity.

Proof

We show that x↦Ux is a bijection from Null(VU) to Null(V)∩Col(U).

Well-defined: If x∈Null(VU), then VUx=0, so Ux∈Null(V). Also Ux∈Col(U) trivially. ✓

Injective: If Ux1=Ux2, then U(x1−x2)=0. Since Null(U)={0} (left cancelable), x1=x2. ✓

Surjective: Take w∈Null(V)∩Col(U). Since w∈Col(U), write w=Ux. Since w∈Null(V), VUx=Vw=0, so x∈Null(VU). ✓

Therefore dim⁡(Null(VU))=dim⁡(Null(V)∩Col(U)). By rank-nullity on the r×r matrix VU:

rank(VU)=r−dim⁡(Null(VU))=r−dim⁡(Null(V)∩Col(U))◻

Remark

Geometric picture: Null(V)∩Col(U) is the "dead zone" — vectors that U produces but V kills. If the overlap is {0}, then VU is invertible.

(c) Verify the formula for the following pair:

U=(100100),V=(100001)
Solution

Col(U)=span{(100),(010)}, Null(V)=span{(010)}.

Overlap: Null(V)∩Col(U)=span{(010)}, dimension 1.

Prediction: rank(VU)=2−1=1.

Verify: VU=(1000). Rank 1. ✓

(d) Now change V=(100010) (same U). What happens?

Solution

Null(V)=span{(001)}. This is not in Col(U), so the overlap is {0}.

Prediction: rank(VU)=2−0=2.

Verify: VU=I2. ✓

Problem 2: Rank of Block Diagonal Matrices ​

Prove:

rank(A00B)=rank(A)+rank(B)

Idea: Cross-fill A and B separately. The rank-one pieces don't interact.

Proof

Cross-fill A=R1+⋯+Rs and B=Q1+⋯+Qt into rank-one pieces. Each Ri embeds as a rank-one piece of the block matrix (padded with zeros in the B rows/columns), and similarly for each Qj. The pieces are supported on disjoint row/column sets, so they don't interact. Total: s+t rank-one pieces. ∎

Example: Compute the rank of (1200240000100001).

Solution

Top-left block: rank 1. Bottom-right block: rank 2. Total rank = 1+2=3. ✓

Problem 3: Invertible Factor Preserves Rank ​

If P is invertible, prove that rank(AP)=rank(A)=rank(PA).

Idea: For AP, use column spaces and the chain Col(AP)⊆Col(A)=Col(APP−1)⊆Col(AP). For PA, use null spaces.

Proof

rank(AP)=rank(A): By Lecture 6 Theorem 1, Col(AP)⊆Col(A). Applying Theorem 1 again with AP in place of A: Col(A)=Col(APP−1)⊆Col(AP). So Col(AP)=Col(A), hence same rank. ∎

rank(PA)=rank(A): By Lecture 6 Theorem 2, Null(A)⊆Null(PA). For the reverse: if PAx=0, left-cancel P to get Ax=0. So Null(PA)=Null(A). Same null space dimension → same rank. ∎

Problem 4: Finding Null(A)∩Col(B) ​

Let A=(102011) and B=(100110).

(a) Find Null(A).

(b) Find Col(B).

(c) Determine whether Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}.

(d) What does this tell you about rank(AB)?

Solution

(a) Solve Ax=0: x1+2x3=0, x2+x3=0. Free variable x3=t:

Null(A)=span{(−2−11)}

(b) Col(B)=span{(101),(010)}

(c) Check if (−2−11)∈Col(B): solve c1(101)+c2(010)=(−2−11).

Row 1: c1=−2. Row 3: c1=1. Contradiction. So Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}. ✓

(d) Since Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}, no columns of B are "wasted" by A. Therefore rank(AB)=rank(B)=2.

Verify: AB=(3011), rank 2. ✓

Problem 5: Rank Inequalities ​

(a) Prove: rank(AB)≤rank(A).

Idea: Col(AB)⊆Col(A).

Proof

By Lecture 6 Theorem 1, Col(AB)⊆Col(A). A subspace contained in another has at most the same dimension:

rank(AB)=dim⁡(Col(AB))≤dim⁡(Col(A))=rank(A)◻

(b) Prove: rank(A+B)≤rank(A)+rank(B).

Idea: Write each matrix as a sum of rank-one pieces via cross-filling.

Proof

Cross-fill A into r rank-one pieces and B into s rank-one pieces. Then A+B is a sum of r+s rank-one matrices. Since each rank-one piece adds at most 1 to the rank:

rank(A+B)≤r+s=rank(A)+rank(B)◻

Problem 6: Null(ATA)=Null(A) ​

Let A be any m×n real matrix.

(a) Prove: Null(ATA)=Null(A).

Idea: One inclusion is immediate. For the other, use ∥Ax∥2=xTATAx.

Proof

(⊇): If Ax=0, then ATAx=AT0=0. ✓

(⊆): If ATAx=0, multiply on the left by xT:

xTATAx=0⟹(Ax)T(Ax)=0⟹∥Ax∥2=0⟹Ax=0◻

(b) Conclude: rank(ATA)=rank(A).

Proof

By rank-nullity, rank(A)=n−dim⁡(Null(A))=n−dim⁡(Null(ATA))=rank(ATA). ∎

TA Notes ​

Time allocation ​

ProblemSuggested TimePriority
Problem 1 (rank of UV and VU)15 minCore — (a) is warm-up, (b) is the main result
Problems 2–3 (block diagonal, invertible factor)8 minState and prove; can abbreviate if short on time
Problem 4 (Null∩Col)10 minLet students try (a)–(c) before showing solution
Problem 5 (rank inequalities)10 minLet students attempt (a) first
Problem 6 (Null(ATA))7 minKey trick: let students find the |⋅|2 idea

Common mistakes ​

  • Confusing UV and VU: UV is m×m (big, not necessarily invertible). VU is r×r (square, can be invertible).
  • rank(AB)≤rank(B): Students often prove ≤rank(A) but forget the transpose trick: apply Col((AB)T)⊆Col(BT).
  • Null(ATA)=Null(A): The ∥⋅∥2 trick only works over R. Students may try to left-cancel AT, which is wrong (not necessarily cancelable).
